collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2024. A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2409.14507 (NeurIPS 2025 Oral)
collisions.overlap: Defines feature absorption, proposes absorption rate metric, validates on hundreds of SAEs. Uses first-letter spelling task exclusively.
collisions.differentiation: Foundational work that the proposal extends. First-letter task only; no cross-domain measurement. No probe quality confound analysis.

collisions.paper: Karvonen et al., 2025. SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders in Language Model Interpretability. arXiv:2503.09532 (ICML 2025)
collisions.overlap: Contains BOTH a feature absorption metric (first-letter) AND a RAVEL disentanglement metric (city-country/continent/language) in the same benchmark suite.
collisions.differentiation: SAEBench treats absorption and RAVEL as independent metrics evaluating different SAE capabilities. Nobody has combined them to compare absorption rates across hierarchy types. The proposal connects these existing ingredients to reveal hierarchy-dependent absorption.

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2025. Feature Hedging: Correlated Features Break Narrow Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2505.11756
collisions.overlap: Studies feature hedging as complementary failure mode; discusses strict vs. loose hierarchy relationships.
collisions.differentiation: Chanin's strict/loose refers to deterministic vs. correlated parent-child relationships. The proposal's strict/compensatory/persistent three-way decomposition is a different concept. The near-tautological finding (0-22.6% strict vs. 92.6% loose) is novel.

collisions.paper: Bussmann et al., 2025. Learning Multi-Level Features with Matryoshka Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2503.17547 (ICML 2025)
collisions.overlap: Reduces absorption via nested hierarchical training; evaluated on SAEBench first-letter absorption metric.
collisions.differentiation: Architecture mitigation paper, not characterization. First-letter evaluation only.

collisions.paper: Korznikov et al., 2025. OrtSAE: Orthogonal Sparse Autoencoders Uncover Atomic Features. arXiv:2509.22033
collisions.overlap: Reduces absorption ~70% via orthogonality penalty; evaluates on SAEBench absorption; measures decoder cosine similarity.
collisions.differentiation: Architecture contribution. First-letter absorption evaluation only.

collisions.paper: Li et al., 2025. Time-Aware Feature Selection: Adaptive Temporal Masking for Stable Sparse Autoencoder Training. arXiv:2510.08855
collisions.overlap: Achieves best reported absorption scores (0.0068); uses first-letter classification benchmark.
collisions.differentiation: Training procedure contribution. First-letter task exclusively.

collisions.paper: Muchane et al., 2025. Incorporating Hierarchical Semantics in Sparse Autoencoder Architectures. arXiv:2506.01197
collisions.overlap: Hierarchical SAE architecture; evaluates on SAEBench first-letter absorption.
collisions.differentiation: Architecture contribution with first-letter eval only. Does not compare absorption across hierarchy types.

collisions.paper: HSAE / From Atoms to Trees, 2026. arXiv:2602.11881
collisions.overlap: Tree-based hierarchical SAE; evaluates on SAEBench absorption; tests cross-model generalizability.
collisions.differentiation: Cross-model (Gemma, Qwen) but not cross-domain absorption. Uses first-letter metric.

collisions.paper: SynthSAEBench, 2026. arXiv:2602.14687
collisions.overlap: Synthetic benchmarks with configurable hierarchical dependencies to study absorption.
collisions.differentiation: Synthetic setting with artificial hierarchies, not real LLM knowledge hierarchies.

collisions.paper: KronSAE, 2025. arXiv:2505.22255
collisions.overlap: Kronecker factorization reduces absorption; first-letter evaluation.

collisions.paper: Looking for Feature Absorption Automatically. LessWrong, 2025
collisions.overlap: Attempts automatic/unsupervised detection of absorption using encoder-decoder discrepancy and causal effect similarity; reports that the method does not work in practice.
collisions.differentiation: Different method (encoder-decoder discrepancy vs. GAS/CMI), convergent negative conclusion. Strengthens the proposal's negative result that unsupervised detection is hard.

collisions.paper: Masked Regularization for SAE Robustness, 2026. arXiv:2604.06495
collisions.overlap: Training-time masking to reduce absorption; very recent (April 2026).

collisions.paper: Resurrecting the Salmon: Domain-Specific SAEs, 2025. arXiv:2508.09363
collisions.overlap: Criticizes first-letter absorption evaluation as 'not useful for evaluating domain-specific feature absorption.' Proposes domain-specific SAE training.
collisions.differentiation: Identifies the same first-letter monoculture problem but proposes domain-specific training as solution, not cross-domain measurement. Validates the proposal's central motivation.

collisions.paper: InverseScope, 2025. arXiv:2506.07406
collisions.overlap: Benchmarks against SAEs on RAVEL dataset for attribute recovery.
collisions.differentiation: Uses RAVEL for activation inversion evaluation, not absorption measurement. Different task.

collisions.paper: Stop Probing, Start Coding, 2026. arXiv:2603.28744
collisions.overlap: Shows SAE dictionary learning is the bottleneck for compositional generalization. Uses controlled experiments isolating dictionary vs. encoder.
collisions.differentiation: Studies compositional generalization failure, not absorption. Different phenomenon. Related decoupling methodology relevant to backup candidate cand_controlled_dictionary.

collisions.paper: Sparse Auto-Encoders and Holism, 2026. arXiv:2603.26207
collisions.overlap: Philosophy paper discussing SAE feature decomposition reliability. Cites absorption as evidence of unreliability.

candidates.differentiation_notes: No published work compares absorption rates across different hierarchy types. The entire absorption evaluation literature uses first-letter spelling exclusively. The proposal's restructured contributions are well-differentiated: (1) Probe degradation methodology has zero prior art -- no one has systematically tested probe quality as an absorption confound. (2) Cross-domain activation patching for competitive exclusion is genuinely new -- Chanin et al. used ablation within first-letter only. (3) SAEBench contains both ingredients but nobody has combined them. The finding that first-letter is not representative would reframe the field.
risks: SAEBench follow-up could extend absorption metric to RAVEL hierarchies before submission
Field is very active; competing cross-domain paper probability increases with delay
'Resurrecting the Salmon' explicitly identifies the first-letter monoculture gap, making it more visible to other researchers

collisions.paper: From Flat to Hierarchical: Extracting Sparse Representations with Matching Pursuit. arXiv:2506.03093
collisions.overlap: MP-SAE uses different encoding strategy but does not hold dictionary constant.

collisions.paper: Stop Probing, Start Coding, 2026. arXiv:2603.28744
collisions.overlap: Isolates dictionary learning vs. encoder amortization as sources of SAE failure. Uses FISTA on frozen SAE dictionaries.
collisions.differentiation: Studies compositional generalization, not absorption. Does not hold dictionary constant for absorption measurement. Related decoupling methodology.

collisions.paper: Compute Optimal Inference and Provable Amortisation Gap, 2024. arXiv:2411.13117
collisions.overlap: Decouples encoder and decoder for inference quality analysis.

candidates.differentiation_notes: First controlled dictionary experiment for absorption. No prior work isolates encoder vs. dictionary contribution to absorption specifically. 'Stop Probing, Start Coding' does related decoupling but for compositional generalization, not absorption.

collisions.paper: A Unified Theory of Sparse Dictionary Learning. arXiv:2512.05534
collisions.overlap: Provides theoretical framework for absorption; different formalism (piecewise biconvex optimization vs. Lotka-Volterra).

candidates.differentiation_notes: Zero prior work on competitive exclusion ecology or phase transitions applied to SAE absorption. Entirely novel framing.

collisions.paper: Masked Regularization for SAE Robustness. arXiv:2604.06495
collisions.overlap: Post-training intervention for absorption; training-time masking vs. inference-time correction.

candidates.differentiation_notes: All existing mitigations are training-time. Inference-time correction is novel.
Natural extension of cross-domain analysis. No specific prior work but relatively straightforward given existing data.

dropped_candidates_novelty.cand_gas_primary: REFUTED experimentally (rho=0.12). No prior published GAS-specific method, but convergent with LessWrong negative finding on unsupervised detection.
dropped_candidates_novelty.cand_cmi_taxonomy: NOT SUPPORTED (rho=0.044, p=0.83). No direct prior work on CMI for absorption, but consistent with the field's difficulty in unsupervised detection.
dropped_candidates_novelty.cand_absorption_tax_quantitative: Quantitative predictions not supported (rho=0.08). Retained as qualitative framework.

key_finding: No published work as of April 15, 2026 compares absorption rates across different hierarchy types. The entire absorption evaluation literature uses the first-letter spelling task exclusively. SAEBench contains both absorption and RAVEL metrics but nobody has combined them. The front-runner's restructured contributions (probe degradation 8/10, causal mechanism 8/10, cross-domain characterization 7/10) present a strong novelty profile. Two new March 2026 papers were identified ('Stop Probing, Start Coding' and 'SAE Holism') but neither overlaps with the core contributions. The primary risk remains that someone combines the existing SAEBench ingredients before submission.
